data.title: Ulta Beauty: Investor Day Unveils Long-Term Value
data.description:
Ulta Beauty (NASDAQ:ULTA) organized its investor day last week, after a tumultuous period. Shares have underperformed recently and ULTA's management is under pressure to deliver. Wall Street seems unsure about this company. Over the past three years, ULTA shares have shifted between outperforming the market and underperforming. There is a lot of information to unpack here.

In my opinion, most investors are too short-term focused, and should look beyond FY2024 and FY2025. I have confidence in the long-term strategy that was presented by ULTA's management, and I rate the company a "Buy".

Investor Day on October 16th

The company shared its long-term strategy, and promised an acceleration towards double-digit EPS growth in 2026 and beyond. However, the weak guidance for FY2024 did not change, and investors did not respond well to this news. This suggests to me that investors are myopically focused on FY2024, instead of FY2026 and beyond. Importantly, as a long-term value investor, I think it's more relevant to think about the long-term growth of the business.

ULTA's long-term vision to have over 1,800 stores in the USA, and they are opening 200 stores in the USA over the next three years. This is about a 15% expansion in their total store count, from the current level of 1,400 stores.

Moreover, ULTA is opening stores in Mexico in 2025 that will contribute to growth and help to establish their franchising model. Over the long term, this franchising infrastructure could be used in other (Latin American) markets.

The double-digit EPS growth target will be achieved by a combination of mid single-digit revenue growth, profit margin expansions, and share buybacks.

Short-term weakness expected in 2024 and 2025

In the short term, however, the guidance is rather weak. For 2024 and 2025, ULTA predicts that it will be underperforming their long-term growth targets. This is mostly due to increased investments, necessary to drive future growth.

“While we expect 2024 and 2025 will be transitional years as we manage through near-term category dynamics, over the long term, we see additional opportunity to expand our leadership position ..."

CFO Paula Oyibo

The stock market makes money flow from the impatient to the patient....

Strategic investments into future growth

These increased investments they're making now will pay off in the future, considering the attractive economics of new store openings with +20% returns. As shown below, ULTA's new stores become more profitable and generate more revenue over time. Becoming value accretive by year 2, and paying back their initial investment in 3 to 4 years. It therefore makes sense to invest more capital in new store openings, considering these great returns.

After a period of increased investment in its digital infrastructure, ULTA is back to focusing on expanding its physical store presence, increasing their stores investments as a percentage of total capital expenditures from 55% in 2022 and 2023 to 70% long-term. These investments in the digital infrastructure were necessary for ULTA to have modern omnichannel capabilities, and these digital services work together with their physical store footprint.

Flywheel of Loyalty

ULTA has the goal to grow their best in-class loyalty program to 50 million members by 2028, up from 40 million members now. According to the presentation, this loyalty program has a +70% retention rate, which is very good. Finally, as shown below, activating these loyalty members across the various touchpoints, like the mobile app or in-store salon, drives more sales.

All of these initiatives are strengthening each other and increase in-store spending. Moreover, Ulta beauty's focus on immersive store experiences with a wide assortment of beauty products and in-store services, helps to differentiate Ulta beauty from general merchandise retailers that have limited offerings, as well as from pure online players such as Amazon.

Capital Allocation

Increased investments back into the business are a good decision in my opinion, and this has been shown to lead to better business performance by Boston Consulting Group as well as others. ULTA still has a lot of potential for growth, so it makes sense to re-invest rather than to pay it out as a dividend.

Considering that ULTA's has very high return on invested capital at about 30%, I feel confident that these investments are going to pay off in the future.

Next to these investments, ULTA announced a $3b share buyback program. They will buy back around $1b of their shares annually, which is around a 6% annual yield for shareholders with their current market cap of around $17b.

EPS estimates and Valuation

Taking these double-digit EPS growth targets into our model, we can estimate the long-term EPS numbers and a fair P/E multiple would be 20. FY2024 guidance is $23 net earnings per share, and FY2025 will be "below long-term targets" but already showing some improvements. Let's assume 5% EPS growth for FY2025 and then 10% EPS growth after that, in line with guidance.

These EPS growth rates imply that the company will be earning over $30 per share in 2027 or 2028. Assuming a 20 times forward P/E ratio, the company could realistically be trading at around $600 per share in a few years time.

The company now trades well below $400 per share, leaving a lot of room for upside potential. Specifically, the latest share price of $368, suggests over 60% upside potential for Ulta Beauty shares if they reach these targets.

Risks

Confidence in these long-term growth rates is questionable, considering that the company does not expect to meet these targets in 2024 and 2025. Management should have a better answer as to why it expects to improve its performance so much in the future, as compared to 2023, 2024, and 2025. If these targets are not met, then these EPS estimates are not accurate.

The Mexican expansion plan could fail, but even if so, this would not be a huge problem for the company because of their capital-light franchising approach.

Conclusion

The investor day provided a lot of useful information about the business and its long-term plans. I feel confident that the company is on the right path, with a solid strategy and a good understanding of their customers. Further expanding their store presence and improving the in-store experience, makes sense to me as strategic priorities to drive more profitable sales.

In conclusion, I would re-iterate my opinion that investors are too focused on the short-term guidance for FY2024 and FY2025. In the long-term, ULTA has a bright future and substantial room to grow. Buying into short-term weakness seems like a good opportunity to me, and I rate the company as a "buy".

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Perplexity AI Targets $8B Valuation In New Funding
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

AI search startup Perplexity (NVDA) aims for $8B valuation in new round. (00:25) UBS sells 50% interest in Swisscard to Amex (AXP) - report. (01:49)Activist investor Starboard takes stake in Kenvue (KVUE). (02:36)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast.

Artificial intelligence search startup Perplexity AI has begun fundraising talks in which it is looking to more than double its valuation to $8 billion or more.

The Wall Street Journal reported Sunday, citing people familiar with the matter, that the Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA)-backed startup has told investors that it is looking to raise about $500 million in the latest funding round.

Perplexity declined to comment.

Perplexity has ambitions to chip away at Google's (GOOG)(GOOGL) dominance in the search arena, utilizing generative AI to answer queries.

The startup does not have its own AI model, and instead uses a combination of existing large language models ((LLMs)), such as OpenAI's ChatGPT and Meta's (META) Llama and builds upon them.

The Jeff Bezos-backed company has started shifting its business model from subscription to advertising, which is more in line with how Google Search generates revenue.

Perplexity's search engine reportedly answered about 250 million questions in July, compared to 500 million throughout the entirety of 2023.

The Wall Street Journal added that the AI startup's estimated annualized revenue based on recent sales is currently about $50 million.

The company has raised three rounds of funding in the past year, with its valuation jumping from $1 billion to $3 billion after its last funding round in May.

UBS (NYSE:UBS) announced the sale of its 50% stake in Swisscard to its joint venture partner American Express (NYSE:AXP),

Reuters reported on Monday that this transaction will make American Express (AXP) the sole owner of Swisscard, which operates credit card services under various brands, including American Express, Mastercard, and Visa.

The specific financial terms of the deal were not disclosed. This sale is part of UBS's (UBS) broader strategy to streamline operations and divest parts of the Credit Suisse business.

UBS confirmed that it was committed to the credit cards business, but issuing credit cards in Switzerland through Swisscard did not "align with the existing operational setup and strategic priorities of UBS as legal successor to Credit Suisse."

Activist investor Starboard Value has amassed a sizable stake in Kenvue Inc. (NYSE:KVUE), the consumer-products company that makes Tylenol and Listerine.

Starboard wants Kenvue, which was spun off from Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) last year, to take measures to help boost its stock price, according to a WSJ report late Sunday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

According to the report, the exact size of Starboard's stake in Kenvue (KVUE) wasn't known and specifics of its plans couldn't be learned.

Kenvue (KVUE) shares are unchanged this year, while the S&P 500 Index has risen 23%.

Kenvue (KVUE) is expected to report Q3 results on Nov. 7.

Kenvue is up more than 5% premarket.

More articles on Seeking Alpha:

Some Apple employees believe it's 2 years behind in AI development: Bloomberg

Honeywell forecast shows increased demand for new business jets

Saudi Arabia's United Electronics plans IPO for finance unit

Catalyst watch:

  • Notable investor event includes Jefferies' (JEF) Investor Day.

  • Qualcomm (QCOM) will hold its Snapdragon Summit in Maui.

Now let’s take a look at the markets as of 6 am. Ahead of the opening bell today, Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 1.5% at $70/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.2% at $68,000.

In the world markets, the FTSE 100 is flat and the DAX is up 0.4%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Spirit Airlines (SAVE) is up 51% after the carrier revealed in a U.S. SEC filing that it has fully utilized its $300M senior secured revolving credit facility, which matures in September 2026.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:55 am Dallas Federal Reserve Bank President Lorie Logan to speak and participate in a moderated question-and-answer session before the 2024 SIFMA (Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association) Annual Meeting.

  • 10:00 am Leading Indicators

  • 6:40 pm San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly Moderated Q&A with the Wall Street Journal's Nick Timiraos at the 2024 WSJ Tech Live.

data.title: Abbott Laboratories: Long-Term Optimism, Short-Term Overvaluation
data.description:
Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT) is a global-diversified healthcare company that develops, manufactures, and sells healthcare products worldwide. It has four main segments: Established Pharmaceutical Products, Diagnostic Products, Nutritional Products, and the largest, Medical Devices. The company has been around since 1888 and has over 114,000 making it an established mega-cap company. As of October 18th, its market capitalization was $205.12 billion, putting it in the top 50 largest US public companies.

ABT is known for being a “Dividend Aristocrat” meaning it is an S&P 500 company that has raised its dividend for a minimum of 25 years. The consistency of its dividend makes it a consistent source of income, and an attractive investment for investors looking for security. However, its dividend yield (TTM) of 1.87% is only about 60 bps higher than the current dividend yield of the S&P 500 ETF (SPY), so that makes it slightly less attractive for income-focused investors. Seeking Alpha gives ABT’s dividend yield (TTM) a B rating because it is somewhat higher than the Health Care sector’s median yield of 1.40%.

Q2 Earnings

ABT announced earnings this past week and reported results that were mostly in-line with expectations. Adjusted EPS beat by a penny at $1.21 per share, while total revenue came in at $10.64 billion, just $84.4 million above Wall Street expectations. This was the third earnings beat in a row. ABT leaned on its strong Medical Devices segment to fuel organic revenue growth of 7.6% YoY (and reported growth of 4.9%) while other segments saw a mixture of slow and moderate growth. For the context of ABT’s earnings, organic growth excludes the impact of foreign exchange, discontinued businesses, and (specifically for the Diagnostics segment) COVID-19 test revenues.

Here is a quick segment breakdown:

  • The Medical Devices segment saw organic growth of 13.3% YoY, with strong growth in both the US and International geographies. ABT’s launch of Lingo, the company’s first continuous glucose monitoring system, boosted the Diabetes Care subsegment sales with a sharp 19.1% YoY increase. Just below that were the Structural Heart (+16.5% YoY) and Heart Failure (+14.4% YoY) subsegments, with several strong-performing products across the board. With Medical Devices accounting for around 45% of the company’s revenues, it is very encouraging to see this segment as the growth leader.
  • The next largest segment, the Diagnostics segment, saw a reported sales decline of -1.5% YoY as the winding down of the COVID-19 crisis into an endemic status caused sales of COVID-19 tests to fall. Organic growth, on the other hand, was positive at 3.3% YoY. Core Laboratory sales growth was a contributor to the organic expansion at 4.5% YoY.
  • The Nutrition segment had some extenuating circumstances affecting Q3 results. First, the winddown of the ZonePerfect product line resulted in some natural runoff of sales there. Additionally, ABT continues to be tied up in lawsuits over its infant formula potentially causing necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC). Through all of that, the segment saw reported sales down -0.3% YoY, but an adjusted organic sales growth rate of 3.4% YoY. ABT’s Ensure and Glucerna products continue to be rock-solid products, driving growth. The Adult subsegment, which includes these two products, saw sales up 9.1% YoY in Q3.
  • The smallest segment, Established Pharmaceuticals, is completely international and represents ABT’s opportunities in emerging markets. This segment was heavily impacted by foreign exchange. The company reported sales up just 2.7% YoY, but adjusted organic growth was 7.0% YoY.

Across all business units, ABT reported total operating costs and expenses up 3.3% YoY, notably slower than the 4.9% YoY increase in reported sales. Cost of goods was up just 2.0% YoY but was outpaced by both R&D expenses up 6.3% YoY and SG&A expenses up 6.3% YoY. With revenue growth outpacing cost growth, ABT saw a strong increase of 12.9% YoY in operating income and an even better net income expansion of 14.6% YoY. However, when adjusted for special after-tax charges, net earnings growth was a slower 5.9% YoY. For the 2024 fiscal year so far, ABT has been able to convert 3.7% YoY revenue growth to a 2.5% YoY increase in adjusted earnings.

ABT also reported that its board authorized a new $7 billion buyback program in Q3 2024 as the program approved in 2021 had been running low. In the last five years, around $5 billion has been allocated towards buybacks. In the past few quarters, share repurchasing had slowed down as ABT completed the acquisition of CSI a year ago, which cost around $826 million in cash as well as other acquisition-related expenses. CEO Robert Ford stated in the earnings call that ABT would start to “step up” repurchases and shared that “about $750 million” was spent on repurchases in Q3.

Valuation

ABT has proven itself to be a reliable cash flow generator that is committed to returning that cash to shareholders through both consistent dividends and share buybacks when it sees that as an efficient allocation of capital. With this in mind, I believe that a dividend growth model with an adjustment for share buybacks is an appropriate method to evaluate ABT’s value.

The first thing to consider is ABT’s long-term growth prospects which are supported by diversification and demographics:

  • The massive healthcare company has a diversified portfolio with several segments growing at different speeds. For example, the Medical Devices segment is growing at a strong rate as it adds successful new products like Lingo and capitalizes on strong established market positions that other products have. This offsets some underperformance in Nutrition and Diagnostics. This diversification supports the assumption that ABT can maintain a steady, but relatively low, growth rate into the future.
  • As a healthcare company, ABT will benefit from the aging of populations in the US and other key developed markets like countries in Europe. The Census Bureau projected that the number of people in the US aged 65 or over will surpass the number of children under 18 by 2034. That imbalance is expected to continue to get worse in the years following. This drastic demographic shift is expected to be a major factor pushing health care spending in the US higher in the next ten years. Research suggests that from 2027-2032, the growth of per capita spending on health care is projected to moderate at an average annual rate of 5.0%. From 2014-2019, that growth averaged just 4.0% per year.

With solid corporate fundamentals and a strong economic case for the long-term growth of ABT, it seems fair to suggest that the company can be described as being in a “steady state” of growth. For my model, I assumed long-term annual nominal growth of 4.4% in earnings based on a return on equity of 15.4% and a payout ratio, through both dividends and share buybacks, of 71.8%. The payout ratio has been estimated using the total of the last six years (2019 to 2024 where I use the most recent TTM data for 2024) of buybacks, dividends, and net long-term debt issued as a percentage of the total net income in the last six years. Similarly, the return on equity has been calculated using the same period.

For the discount rate, I calculate the cost of equity using a beta (trailing 24 months) of 0.51, an equity risk premium of 3.9%, and a risk-free rate of 3.8% (ERP and risk-free rate both come from Aswath Damodaran). The projection of next year's total payout per share assumes GAAP EPS growth of 4.4% YoY and a 71.8% payout ratio (both in line with long-term estimates). The result of this constructed dividend growth model, which accounts for share buybacks, suggests that shares of ABT are valued at around $171.69 which means that the market is currently undervaluing shares of ABT with a price of just under $120.

Discussion

The simple model devised above has some limitations that should be recognized:

  • The model assumes GAAP EPS growth in 2024 and 2025 as the company is working through declines in COVID-19 testing sales and some negative impacts from discontinuing products. I do believe that growth in the Medical Devices segment can offset these declines, but that may be too optimistic.
  • A payout ratio that considers both dividends and buybacks may be difficult to estimate since recent results have been impacted by acquisitions and, of course, the pandemic. ABT has a very consistent dividend, but its trends in stock buybacks have been more volatile. Overall, smoothing out the data for the past five years seems like the most sensible thing to do.

Looking at a quick sensitivity analysis, we see that if ABT’s EPS growth in 2025 were to be less or none at all and the payout ratio were to increase as a result of lower earnings, the estimated value of the stock would be lower.

From a more simplified valuation perspective, using the P/E and EV/EBITDA ratios, shares of ABT have higher valuations. The Non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 25.6x is slightly above the sector median and is assigned a C by Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating System. The forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 20.2x gets a C- rating for being about 45% higher than the sector median. ABT’s stock at this valuation is not absurd as the demographic driver of growth is strong, and the company’s portfolio is robust. However, investors shouldn’t expect an explosive expansion from ABT any time soon which is why there is a focus on returning cash to shareholders.

Conclusion

ABT’s Q2 earnings were a strong signal to investors that its strongest segment is still strong, and that it is leading the way as other business units face some sluggishness from the drawing down of the COVID-19 pandemic cash flows and sales from restructuring. An optimistic perspective suggests that the stock has good long-term prospects and can return cash to investors while maintaining a low, steady growth rate, and in that context, the stock is undervalued. However, the market is currently more cautious on growth, and perhaps, has already priced in the effects of the new $7 billion buyback program. I assign a Hold rating to ABT which is in line with the Quant Rating System grade, and in line with other analysts who see valuations as a bit stretched at the moment.

data.title: SRV: Still Looking Like A Solid Midstream Investment
data.description:
The NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund (NYSE:SRV) is a closed-end fund that investors can purchase as a way to obtain access to a portfolio of midstream corporations and master limited partnerships. This could be an appealing proposition for someone who wishes to include companies such as these inside of a tax-advantaged individual retirement account or similar vehicle. After all, there can be tax problems when attempting to include a partnership in a retirement account that could result in an investor having to pay taxes on income that their retirement account receives. This fund solves that problem by covering the taxes on a fund level and paying 1099 dividends like a regular corporation. This fund also manages to boast a considerably higher yield than most master limited partnerships, which adds to its appeal.

As of the time of writing, the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund boasts an 11.48% yield. As we can see here, this is considerably higher than any of the fund's peers:

This is something that will almost certainly appeal to most potential investors in this fund. After all, one of the biggest reasons why an investor may wish to include midstream corporations or master limited partnerships in their portfolios is because of the high yields that these companies typically possess. In short, many investors looking at an investment in this sector will probably want income, so they will frequently prefer a yield that is as high as realistically possible. This fund definitely satisfies that desire and so could be attractive in this respect.

However, as I pointed out in a recent article:

The fund's high yield could also be a sign that the market doubts its ability to sustain the payout going forward. The market has a tendency to price a fund's shares at a level that will give it a competitive yield after it cuts to the level that the market expects it to pay, after all.

Thus, the fact that this fund's yield is so much higher than its peers could be worrisome. We will want to pay close attention to its finances and net asset value development in order to determine whether or not it is truly able to continue to maintain the payout at the current level. We will do this over the remainder of this article.

As regular readers might remember, we previously discussed the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund in early August of this year. The equity market has generally been very strong since that time, driven by monetary easing cycles coming out of Europe, Canada, and eventually the United States. There has also been some uncertainty regarding the war in the Middle East and forecasts that this coming winter may be colder than the previous two were, which has provided some support for crude oil and natural gas prices. The companies in which this fund is invested do not technically benefit from rising energy prices, but the market price of their common equity securities sometimes does. Due to these factors, we might expect the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund to have delivered a relatively decent performance since our last discussion.

This is certainly the case, as shares of this fund have risen by 10.82% since our previous discussion:

As we can clearly see in the chart above, the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund managed to outperform the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the period. This is no small feat for a midstream fund, as the sector generally underperformed the market for the decade preceding the COVID-19 pandemic. Subscribers to Energy Profits in Dividends are well aware that the sector has improved significantly since that time, and this is generally reflected in the market price performance of many companies in the sector. Indeed, as we can see here, the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund has beaten the S&P 500 Index over the past three years and the Alerian MLP Index (AMLP) managed to come pretty close:

This certainly reflects well on this fund. After all, we all like to outperform the broader market index. It also clearly shows that the midstream sector should no longer be written off by investors, as many did during the period of very disappointing performance around the middle of the last decade.

Investors in the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund actually did a lot better than the above chart suggests, however. As I stated in my previous article on this fund:

A simple look at a closed-end fund's price performance does not necessarily provide an accurate picture of how investors in the fund did during a given period. This is because these funds tend to pay out all of their net investment profits to the shareholders, rather than relying on the capital appreciation of their share price to provide a return. This is the reason why the yields of these funds tend to be much higher than the yield of index funds or most other market assets.

We should, therefore, include the distributions that were paid out by this fund as part of our performance analysis. When we do that, we get the following performance chart for the period from August 2, 2024 (the date of my previous article) through today:

This seems quite certain to satisfy any potential investor who is considering an investment in the fund. As we can immediately see, the fund outperformed both the Alerian MLP Index and the broader large-cap index. This is certainly something that we want to see. However, the fact that this fund outperformed the Alerian MLP Index to the extent that it did do beg questions. In particular, it is possible that the share price of this fund substantially outperformed the underlying portfolio, which means that its valuation may be less attractive than we would really like to see. We will want to have a look at this later in today's article.

As approximately two-and-a-half months have passed since our last discussion on the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund, it would be reasonable to assume that some things have changed. The remainder of this article will focus specifically on these changes on both a fund level and in the broader macroeconomic environment.

About The Fund

According to the fund's website, the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of after-tax total return. This objective makes sense given the fund's strategy, which is described thusly on the website:

The Fund's investment objective is to obtain a high after-tax total return from a combination of capital appreciation and current income. The Fund seeks to achieve its investment objective by investing, under normal conditions, at least 80% of its net assets, plus any borrowings for investment purposes, in midstream energy investments.

The fund's most recent annual report offers a better description of the fund's strategy:

The Fund seeks to achieve its investment objective by investing, under normal market conditions, at least 80% of Managed Assets (as defined in this report) in a portfolio of midstream energy investments ("the 80% policy"). For purposes of the Fund's 80% policy, midstream energy investments are investments that offer economic exposure to securities of midstream energy companies, which are companies that provide midstream energy services, including the gathering, transporting, processing, fractionation, storing, refining and distribution of natural resources, such as natural gas, natural gas liquids, crude oil refined petroleum products, biofuels, carbon sequestration, solar, and wind. The Fund considers a company to be a midstream energy company if at least 50% of its assets, income, sales or profits are committed to or derived from midstream energy services.

The Fund invests in equity and debt securities of midstream energy companies, and invests in U.S. and non-U.S. securities and in issuers of any market capitalization size.

As an alternative to holding investments directly, the Fund may obtain investment exposure through derivatives transactions intended to replicate, modify or replace the economic attributes associated with investment in securities in which the Fund is permitted to invest directly. To the extent that the Fund invests in synthetic investments with economic characteristics similar to investments in midstream energy companies, the market value (or, if market value is unavailable, the fair value) of such investments will be counted for purposes of the Fund's policy of investing at least 80% of its Managed Assets in a portfolio of midstream energy investments.

This latter description tells us a few things that the website does not. Perhaps most importantly, the second paragraph of the annual report's description states that the fund can invest in both equity and debt securities as long as they are issued by a midstream company. This is important because equity and debt securities have different characteristics. Debt securities are mostly priced based on interest rates and the market's perception of the company's ability to pay back the debt in full. Equity securities, on the other hand, depend more directly on the company's financial performance and its growth prospects. Equity securities, therefore, have substantially greater potential to deliver capital gains and will usually deliver a higher total return over the long term. As regular readers know, I doubt the current narrative that inflation is "beaten" and I would not be at all surprised to see it take off again as the Federal Reserve continues to reduce its benchmark interest rate. In such an environment, equity securities are vastly superior to debt securities due to the fact that they should appreciate in price as inflation pushes the issuing company's revenues and net income upward. Debt securities, meanwhile, may struggle to provide any real return.

Fortunately, then, the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund is primarily invested in equities. As we saw in the previous article, the fund's semi-annual report provides the following asset allocation as of May 31, 2024:

This fund has not released an updated holding report as of the time of writing, so we cannot really see how this has changed since then. However, we can look at all of the fund's financial documents over the past year and attempt to identify a pattern:

(all figures are as a percentage of net assets)

The NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund had no debt securities in its portfolio at all over the past year, except for the U.S. Treasury bills that might be held by the money market funds. Rather, its portfolio consists almost entirely of common equities in most periods. It tends to have a small allocation to preferred stock, but this is less than 3% of the fund's portfolio, so it should not affect its overall performance by very much. Clearly, we can see that this fund should behave like an equity fund, and as such it should offer the inflation protection that such a fund would ordinarily provide.

At this point, some readers might point out that preferred stocks typically have higher yields than common stock of the same issuer. Indeed, as brokerage firm Saxo states on its website:

Preferred stocks typically offer higher dividend yields than common stocks and are often higher than bonds, reflecting their intermediate risk level. These yields compensate for their position in the capital structure, ranking below bonds but above common stocks in claims on assets during liquidation.

Thus, usually investing in common equities as opposed to preferred stock lowers the level of income that an investment will provide. However, this is not necessarily true for midstream partnerships. We can see this by looking at the yields of the largest positions in the fund. Here they are:

For some reason, this fund is not providing holding data more recent than June 30, 2024. This is very disappointing as most funds, even those that only update their websites quarterly, have holding data from the end of September published. This, therefore, means that we cannot provide a complete update on the fund's holding, since this is the same chart as was available when we looked at this fund back in August.

3.63%

ONEOK (OKE)

4.07%

As of right now, the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF), which tracks domestic preferred stock, has a thirty-day SEC yield of 5.96%. We can therefore see that several of the companies on this list, particularly those that are structured as master limited partnerships, have higher yields than the preferred stock index. However, the yield of an individual company's preferred stock might be higher or lower than the index, depending on the market's perception of that particular security's level of risk. Here are the preferred stock holdings listed in the semi-annual report:

The fund's documents do not state whether the NGL Energy Partners are Class B or Class C preferreds, which is disappointing. The two issues are almost identical in terms of price and yield, however:

Issue Name

Current Price

Current Yield

NGL Energy Partners LP Pfd Unit Class B (NGL.PR.B)

$23.50

13.62%

NGL Energy Partners LP 9.625 Pfd Unit C (NGL.PR.C)

$23.15

13.73%

The Class C preferred units were trading at $24.46 on May 31, 2024, which is the date listed on the semi-annual report. That perfectly correlates to the 20,313 units that the fund held on that date. Thus, there is definitely a good reason to believe that the fund is holding the NGL Energy Partners Class C Preferred Units, but it does not explicitly state this. These units have a considerably higher yield than any of the common equity held in the fund's largest positions.

The Algonquin Power & Utilities (AQN) preferreds and the NextEra Energy (NEE) preferreds certainly appear to have lower yields, though. The fund does not explicitly state what series of preferreds these are either, which makes it difficult to get exact figures. The current yield of Algonquin Class A preferreds is 7.27% while the yield of NextEra Energy Class R preferreds (NEE.PR.R) is 7.42% at the current price. It is uncertain whether or not these are the exact issues that the fund is holding, but it would be unusual for the preferred issues from the same company to have radically different yields. Thus, we can conclude that the fund's utility preferreds are probably yielding somewhere between 7.25% and 7.50% right now. That is better than the fund's midstream corporation common stock is yielding, but it is not really an improvement over the common equity of the master limited partnerships.

In short, we can see that choosing common equities over preferred stock is not really costing the fund as much income as would be assumed. The common equities are also much better positioned to give capital gains, which is exactly what we want if we are hoping to beat inflation.

It is pretty much certain that we will see at least one major change the next time that the fund releases its holdings information. This is because Equitrans Midstream Corporation no longer exists as an independent company. It was acquired by EQT Corporation (EQT) in July and stopped trading as an independent company at that time. As such, that company is not in the fund's top ten holdings anymore, and it will be gone the next time that NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund releases its holdings. It is uncertain what will replace it, as it seems unlikely that the fund will have held on to the shares of EQT Corporation that it got via the acquisition. After all, EQT does not meet the fund's definition of a midstream company for inclusion in a fund (as detailed above in the quote).

Leverage

As is the case with most closed-end funds, the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund employs leverage as a method of boosting the effective total return and yield that it earns from the assets in its portfolio. I explained how this works in the previous article on this fund:

In short, the fund is borrowing money and using that borrowed money to purchase the common equity of midstream companies. As long as the purchased assets have a higher total return than the interest rate that the fund needs to pay on the borrowed monies, the strategy works pretty well to boost the effective yield of the portfolio. As this fund is capable of borrowing money at institutional rates, which are considerably lower than retail rates, this will usually be the case. It is worth noting that this strategy is much less effective at boosting the effective portfolio return today than it was three years ago. This is simply because borrowing money is considerably more expensive today than it used to be.

However, the use of debt in this fashion is a double-edged sword. This is because leverage boosts both gains and losses. As such, we want to ensure that the fund is not using too much leverage because that would expose us to an excessive amount of risk. I generally do not like to see a fund's leverage exceed a third as a percentage of its assets for this reason.

According to CEF Data, the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund currently has leveraged assets comprising 29.23% of its overall portfolio. This is almost perfectly in line with what the fund had the last time that we discussed it, which is rather surprising. After all, as we saw in the introduction, the shares of this fund are up 10.82% since the last time that we discussed the fund. That should mean that the fund's portfolio has increased in size since that time, so all else being equal, its leverage should increase.

The fund's portfolio did increase in size during the interim period, albeit not as much as the fund's share price. This chart shows the fund's net asset value from August 2, 2024 (the date of the previous article) through today:

As we can see, the fund's portfolio has increased by 8.87% since our previous discussion. If its outstanding borrowings remained the same, then the fund's leverage should now represent a smaller proportion of a larger portfolio. The fact that the fund's leverage is identical to what it was the last time that we discussed this fund suggests that it may have borrowed more money to boost its gains as midstream companies rallied.

The fund is still well below the one-third of assets level that we generally consider to be acceptable for an equity fund. However, as I noted in my previous article:

The NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund is more highly leveraged than all but one of its peers. This might be concerning considering that some of these funds have had problems with leverage in the past. After all, leverage basically caused these funds to wipe themselves out in 2015 and 2020 when the sector collapsed. While the sector is now much stronger than it has ever been, there is still the fear that such an event could happen again. That is why most of these funds have lower levels of leverage than they used to. This fund obviously does not, and as such it could be riskier than some of its peers in the event of a sudden near-term correction.

Here is how the fund's leverage compares with its peers today:

Fund Name

Leverage Ratio

NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund

29.23%

ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity Fund

28.35%

Kayne Anderson Energy Infrastructure Fund

22.03%

Neuberger Berman Energy Infrastructure and Income Fund

17.07%

Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corp.

20.20%

(all figures from CEF Data)

The last time that we discussed this fund, the ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity Fund was the only one that had a higher level of leverage. Today, we can see that things have changed, as that fund now has a slightly lower level of leverage than the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund. This does, unfortunately, mean that this is the riskiest fund out of the group. That does not necessarily mean that it is likely to get into problems, but those investors who are highly risk-averse will likely want to keep this in mind.

Distribution Analysis

In the previous article on this fund, we saw that the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund fully covered its distribution for the first half of its current fiscal year, which corresponds to the six-month period that ended on May 31, 2024. The fund has not released a more recent financial report, so there is no point in repeating the entire section. However, we do still want to make sure that it is still covering the distribution.

This chart shows the fund's net asset value from May 31, 2024, through today. As such, it covers the entire period that has passed since the closing date of the fund's most recent financial report:

As we can see, the fund's net asset value has risen by 5.45% since the financial report closed. This is exactly what we want, as we want the fund to be distributing less than it generates in investment profits. A declining net asset value represents an unsustainable distribution, and we see the exact opposite here. Thus, for the time being, the fund appears to be in pretty good shape.

It continues to be true that investors should not need to worry about the fund's distribution.

Valuation

Shares of the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund are currently trading at an enormous 6.48% premium to net asset value. This is higher than the 5.76% premium that the shares have averaged over the past month. It is also higher than the discounts possessed by most midstream closed-end funds. As such, this is one of the most expensive funds in the sector right now.

Expenses

The NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund has a 4.39% expense ratio, which will undoubtedly seem expensive to many readers. However, the majority of these expenses are due to the carrying costs of the fund's leverage. If we exclude these costs, the fund still has a 1.93% expense ratio, so it is not cheap, but it is not as expensive as it appears at first.

Here is how the fund compares to its peers:

(all figures from CEF Data)

The NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund is the most expensive out of its peer group, but this is largely due to the fact that it uses more leverage than comparable funds. The fund is not the most expensive if we exclude its leverage, though, but it is still close. This may be off-putting to many potential investors, but the fund's performance justifies the high expenses as far as I am concerned.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund continues to look like one of the best midstream funds around. The fund's managers could do a much better job of communicating with its investors, though, as the website is more than three months out of date. Most funds are somewhat more prompt with providing portfolio information. Other than that, though, this fund appears to be a good source of income, and it is surprisingly fully covering its payout. This fund is definitely a buy if it ever starts trading at a discount.
