data.title: FYLD: A Comprehensive Measure For Yield Brought To International Markets
data.description:
I’m a fan of Meb Faber, the man behind Cambria. He’s a thoughtful manager who has a lot of great research he’s turned into different funds. He’s become more known over the last several years for his approach towards shareholder yield, and is applying that concept to foreign equities through the Cambria Foreign Shareholder Yield ETF (BATS:FYLD). Shareholder yield is a comprehensive measure that takes into account dividends, net share buybacks, and net debt reduction. By doing this, FYLD goes a step further than typical dividend investing. It considers several ways companies can give value back to their shareholders. FYLD aims to find companies with strong free cash flow and management teams that put shareholders first.

A Look At The Holdings

This is a nicely diversified fund. No position currently makes up more than 1.21% of the fund.

So what do these companies do? Shibaura Mechatronics Corporation makes and sells high-tech gear. This includes equipment to make FPDs (Flat Panel Displays), semiconductors, and vacuum systems. They also produce other special industrial machines. BPER Banca SPA stands as a top Italian bank group. They offer many financial services. These cover corporate and investment banking, private banking, wealth management, and bancassurance. They also provide leasing, factoring, and retail lending. The bank has branches all over Italy and in Luxembourg. Imperial Oil Ltd operates as a complete Canadian oil company. They look for, produce, refine, and sell crude oil and natural gas. They also make and sell petrochemicals. And Shougang Fushan Resources Group Ltd is a Chinese coal mining firm. They own and run three coking coal mines in Shanxi Province. They dig up, process, and sell top-quality hard coking coal and semi-hard coking coal. Their main customers are big steelmakers in China.

These main investments show FYLD's focus on businesses with healthy cash flows that give back value to shareholders in different industries and countries.

Sector and Global Allocation

The sector mix has some nice overweights and underweights relative to the more traditional EAFE Index. Financials make up the largest allocation, followed by Energy and Industrials.

This sector allocation leans toward value-oriented sectors that often produce big cash flows and have a track record of giving capital back to shareholders.

As to global allocation, Japan is the largest allocation, followed by Canada and Britain. We can see the allocations here again are solidly different from EAFE.

Peer Comparison

Unlike ETFs that cover a wide range of international stocks such as the iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA) or the Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets ETF (VEA), FYLD focuses in on companies that give high returns to shareholders. When we look at the price ratio of FYLD to EFA, we find that FYLD has outperformed EAFE over the past 5 years, though it has underperformed since mid-2022 – around the time the stock market bottomed and Tech took the leadership role. The underweight here probably hurt the fund on a relative basis.

One key feature of FYLD is how it leans towards small and medium-sized stocks, with about 39% of its portfolio in big-name companies. This is different from many international funds that put a lot of weight on large-cap stocks and might give you a look at a different part of the market.

Pros and Cons

On the plus side, FYLD's attention to shareholder yield offers a different way to invest. By looking at dividends, share buybacks, and debt reduction, the fund tries to spot companies that make money and also give value back to shareholders. This can be appealing when yields are low and investors want to earn income from their stock investments. The fund's hands-on management offers another possible edge. In the tricky and always-changing world of global markets, having pros like Meb and his team who can tweak the portfolio as things shift can help. This ability to adapt might let FYLD handle market ups and downs better than a fund that just follows an index. FYLD's focus on small and medium-sized companies could also be a plus, as these firms often have more space to grow than big ones.

But we should also think about the possible drawbacks. The fund's emphasis on shareholder yield can result in sector concentrations, as shown by the big stakes in Financials and Energy. These sectors often give good yields, but they can also underperform meaningfully in Tech-driven rallies, as we’ve seen over the past two years. The fund's focus on international markets puts investors at risk from currency changes as well. Shifts in exchange rates can affect returns in the short run.

Keep in mind that companies with high shareholder yields aren't always the fastest-growing businesses. By concentrating on companies that give cash back to shareholders, FYLD might underweight companies that grow and put a lot of money back into their operations. This could result in weaker performance when growth stocks are popular.

Conclusion

I’m a fan of FYLD. It’s an ETF that is potentially great for those who want to access international stocks that prioritize shareholder-friendly practices. Its distinctive strategy to consider dividends, stock buybacks, and debt reduction gives a full picture of how firms return value to their shareholders. If you’re looking for a solid international diversifier, this fund is one to consider.

data.title: Netflix Q3 Earnings: Paid Memberships Growth Peaked, Expect Growth Normalize
data.description:
3Q FY2024 Earnings Takeaway

Netflix (NEOE:NFLX:CA) (NASDAQ:NFLX) delivered another solid earnings report in 3Q FY2024, driven by robust growth in paid net adds, beating both revenue and non-GAAP EPS consensus. In my 2Q earnings analysis, I maintained a buy rating on the stock, supported by accelerating growth in paid memberships. NFLX is well-positioned to maintain its current industry leadership. Since my last rating, the stock has rallied 15%, beating the S&P 500 index by 10%.

However, NFLX's current valuation is not cheap, reflecting an expected 60% YoY EPS growth in FY2024, driven by record operating margin and mid-teens revenue growth. While the company guided a better-than-expected revenue outlook and indicated that margin expansion will continue into FY2025, I believe the paid memberships growth has peaked in 2Q FY2024, and the operating margin will normalize in FY2025 to balance future growth.

Moreover, the management also noted that ads will not be a primary driver of revenue in FY2025 as the company is currently focusing on scaling audiences other than monetization. Therefore, I believe the stock is pricing for perfection in the near term, and therefore a hold rating on NFLX is warranted. However, I acknowledge there may be some near-term consolidation, which could create an attractive entry point.

Paid Memberships Growth Has Peaked

NFLX's 3Q total paid memberships continued to show resilience, growing 14.4% YoY. As shown in the chart, the growth trajectory is starting to normalize as paid net adds decelerated in 3Q. It's important to note that a slowdown in paid net adds doesn't mean the company has stopped growing its paid memberships base. 3Q paid net adds were largely driven by EMEA and APAC regions, while the UCAN region saw some sequential softness, and LATAM experienced negative paid net adds for the first time since 1Q FY2023.

During the 3Q earnings call, management attributed the paid net loss in LATAM to "recent price changes in some of our larger LATAM markets." However, in the Regional Breakdown section, we notice that the average revenue per membership in 3Q FY2024 was even lower than in 3Q FY2023, suggesting that the lower demand wasn't driven by price hikes as seen in UCAN. They also mentioned that LATAM's paid net adds would have been positive if the quarter had lasted one more day. I believe this "channel stuffing" explanation is not convincing, and any potential rebound in 4Q should be discounted accordingly.

My counterargument to the bullish thesis for NFLX is that its paid net adds will begin to normalize in FY2025, signaling a significant slowdown in total paid memberships (anticipate mid-single digit), as seen in the post-pandemic era, which could impact its revenue growth.

Balanced Margin Expansion In FY2025

NFLX has been steadily improving its operational efficiency and is expected to continue expanding its operating margin in FY2025. In my last analysis, I highlighted that the company achieved a record operating margin of 28.1% in 1Q FY2024. In 3Q FY2024, the company set a new record, reaching 29.6%.

Management has guided an operating margin of 22% for 4Q, with the sequential drop reflecting typical seasonality, as seen in previous fourth quarters. However, the 4Q margin still shows significant improvement compared to the same period over the past two fiscal years. It's impressive how the company is navigating rising costs while expanding into new areas such as live-streaming, ads, and gaming. I believe most of these businesses are still unprofitable. Despite an anticipated 6.4% increase in operating margin in FY2024, the company is also expected to improve operating margin by another 1% in FY2025. During the earnings call, NFLX indicated that as its growth normalizes in FY2025, the company plans to balance near-term margin expansion with reinvestments in new growth opportunities.

Strong Earnings Growth Boosts FCF

NFLX's earnings growth has been strong throughout FY2024. The company reported a 45% YoY increase in diluted EPS in 3Q, driven by solid revenue growth and a record-high operating margin. For 4Q, as mentioned, its operating margin is expected to be 5% higher than the same period last fiscal year, which will significantly improve its earnings outlook, though specific guidance was not provided. According to Seeking Alpha's earnings consensus, the company is expected to generate $19.80 in EPS for FY2024, implying a 4Q EPS of $4.24 and 101% YoY growth. FY2024 EPS is projected to grow 60.6% YoY, marking a significant acceleration from 20.4% YoY growth in FY2023.

Due to strong earnings growth, NFLX's FCF increased by 19.4% YoY in 3Q FY2024. Despite more than tripling its FCF in FY2023, I believe the company's FCF could be even higher in FY2024, even with rising capex. Lastly, the management has no intention of increasing leverage to fund stock buybacks or issue dividends in the near term.

Valuation

I previously noted that NFLX could be trading at higher valuation multiples due to its strong revenue growth acceleration and margin expansion. While its non-GAAP P/E fwd is currently below its 5-year average, the stock had been trading at over 40x on average during that period. This indicated that the stock might have been ahead of itself and could normalize as earnings grow. Its current multiple is around 38.6x, which makes me cautious, especially as its growth on paid memberships growth begins to decelerate.

Moreover, its EV/sales TTM has been trending higher despite strong revenue TTM growth, and the multiple is now 30% above its 5-year average. In my previous analysis, I noted that its non-GAAP PEG fwd was 21.5% below the sector average, according to Seeking Alpha. Currently, this multiple is trading at 1.46x, which is 8.2% above the sector average. Therefore, I'm lowering my rating on the stock from buy to hold for now.

Conclusion

In summary, NFLX has demonstrated its industry leadership as indicated by its strong revenue growth, margin expansion, and robust FCF generation in FY2024. However, its paid membership growth peaked in 2Q FY2024 and started to decelerate in 3Q, as growth is expected to normalize while the margin will increase modestly in FY2025. We noticed that some region locations have shown negative net adds in the last quarter, even though it might prove to be short-lived. Other parts of the growth driver like ads and live-streaming may not significantly contribute to revenue growth in the near term. Therefore, its valuation multiples are becoming a point of concern as growth normalizes. The forward P/E trading near 40x adds to valuation risks, increasing the potential for multiple contractions when the economy slows. Therefore, I downgraded the rating to hold to reflect my cautious outlook.

data.title: CeriBell: An Interesting EEG Play Going Public
data.description:
Shares of CeriBell (NASDAQ:CBLL) have seen a very successful public offering, as shares have risen some sixty percent from the official public offering price.

The company has developed and commercializes an interesting new EEG platform (solution), which sees solid growth, but comes with continued losses for now.

I think that CeriBell Is a very interesting business, and I am willing to keep a close eye on the developments from here, but I am not yet willing to get involved just yet, certainly after a big share price run post the offering.

Clarity When It Is Critical

CeriBell is a commercial stage medical technology business which focuses on the diagnosis and management of patients which suffer from neurological conditions.

The CeriBell System is a point-of-care electroencephalography (EEG) platform which provides rapid diagnosis and continued monitoring of patients suffering from such conditions. The company focuses on the acute care setting, in which traditional EEG has serious drawbacks.

The company believes that traditional EEG systems, designed over a century ago, suffer from slow speed of diagnosis, no continued monitoring, while they are often operated by staff, making them labor-intensive. These drawbacks are very easily understood, as any seizure lasting for more than 5 minutes obtains the status of epilepticus, a serious medical conditions which can lead to mortality or serious brain damage.

With the CeriBell being easily put into use, even by non-trained professionals, there is continued monitoring and quick diagnosis, which obviously helps patients in a huge way, but also improves hospitals economics and addresses labor shortages in the health field.

Founded in 2014, CeriBell obtained FDA approval in 2017, as the CeriBell System saw the start of commercialization in 2018. Ever since, the company has grown to employ over 200 workers, with a sales base of 70 workers trying to sell these solutions to nearly 6,000 acute care facilities across the US.

The business model is quite interesting, consisting out of a $799 hardware fee for the disposable headband, as well as a $5,000 monthly subscription fee being charged for recorders and portals.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

CeriBell aimed to sell 10.6 million shares in a preliminary price range between $16 and $17 per share, with final pricing set at the higher end of the range. This made that gross proceeds were seen around $180 million, with no shares sold by existing shareholders.

With a total share count of just over 34 million shares, the company was valued at $578 million, or just over $400 million factoring in the pro forma net cash holdings.

The company generated sales of $25.9 million in 2022 on which the company reported a huge operating loss of $36.0 million. Revenues rose by a spectacular 75% to $45.2 million in 2023 as operating losses narrowed to $30.0 million, indicating that the company made progress in absolute, but certainly in relative terms.

In the first half of this year, the company grew sales by another 45% to $29.7 million, running at a $60 million run rate. Contrary to 2023, operating loses actually increased a bit from $14.5 million to $16.9 million for the six-month period.

Preliminary third quarter sales are seen between $16.9 and $17.2 million, yet losses from operations are seen at a midpoint of $10 million, actually being on the increase. Third quarter sales are seen up 47% and now trend near $70 million, yet losses are no longer coming down, trending at $40 million per annum. Fortunately, the company has about $170 million in pro forma net cash upon the public offering, making it easy for the company to sustain losses for some time to come.

Trading at $27 following the IPO, the market value of the firm has risen above $900 million, for an operating asset valuation of around three quarters of a billion. With revenues trending near $70 million per annum, operating assets are trading around 10 times sales. This is demanding, but this comes amidst near 50% revenue growth rates, as well as serious losses, of course.

Concluding Thoughts

The truth is that CeriBell looks like an interesting business, one with an interesting product (proposition), as the financials make it clear that the company enjoys solid adoption of its products, as demonstrated by the rapid growth on the topline.

While this revenue growth is solid, the company is still posting losses here, which have been coming down in 2023, a trend which has reversed so far in 2024 as losses are no longer coming down (on an absolute basis). Fortunately, the company has sufficient cash to withstand these losses for some time to come.

In this trade-off, I am seeing the positives. This includes a current market value of less than a billion, a huge market opportunity, rapid growth demonstrated upon, and compelling health improvements delivered upon for patients and healthcare professionals, all indicating that the potential is very much there. On the other hand, there is a high current demanding valuation at 10 times sales, losses being incurred, the risks to rely on a single product, reimbursement risks, an evolving competitive landscape, among many others.

Amidst all this, I am cautious to get involved just yet, certainly after shares have seen gains to the tune of 60% in the first days of trading. Right now, I am attracted to the business and keen to keep a close eye on the developments from here, yet see no reason to get involved with the stock at current levels just yet.

data.title: Lumen: Turnaround Gaining Steam But This Stock Ain't Cheap
data.description:
Lumen Technologies (NYSE:LUMN) shareholders have had an eventful few months as the stock has gone from being priced at high risk for bankruptcy due to declining revenues in core segments and a large debt burden, to a valuation explosion on the back of improved cash flow from being part of the AI growth wave and increasingly optimistic hopes for a successful turnaround. While the company is certainly in a better position now than a few months ago, many risks remain and investors should tread carefully.

If You Build It ...

Just a few months ago, LUMN was worth about $1.5 billion, had an over-leveraged balance sheet, a major liquidity gap to cover interest expenses and principal payments, and had gradually declining cash flows and core revenues. Now the stock is worth $6.8 billion, cash flow is improving, and the company is gearing up for a growth phase. What the heck happened?

It's a really fascinating example of how investing in infrastructure can pay dividends in unexpected ways in the future beyond the initial intended uses. Much of Lumen's current communications network was built over the last quarter-century during a time when AI was more science fiction antagonist than realistic technology of the future, and yet, when the company's future was looking increasingly grim, those investments seem to have paid off. CFO Chris Stansbury had this to say at a Goldman Sachs conference on communications technology:

So much of it really rides on the fact that over the years and really many, many years ago, 25 years ago, in fact, this large intra and intercity network of conduit was built. And the beautiful thing about conduit is you can quickly deploy fiber through those tubes. And speed is the resource that these large companies don't have right now time, right?

I don't mean to discount the foresight of whoever originally had the idea to greenlight this investment, but its monetization certainly came at the perfect time. Now hyperscalers chasing the AI revolution need a way to connect their data centers across distances vast and small, and Lumen is prepared to lease them the data highways to do it. That's how a stock gains 400% in a couple months' time!

... They Will Come

The major drivers of recent gains have been an agreement with Corning (GLW) to use 10% of its fiber capacity in Lumen's conduit network to interconnect AI-enabled data centers, the securing of $5 billion in fiber service capacity deals for these data centers, and an additional $7 billion in potential deals that are still in "active discussion". These have caused the stock to rally, not only because other companies serving the AI wave have seen their share prices appreciate considerably, but because Lumen had been in a bit of a death spiral in regards to its balance sheet for a few years now before these most recent developments. Revenue and cash flow have been in a gradual decline even as the company's $18.4 billion in long-term debt and 7.74% interest rate as of the most recent quarter loom large and weigh on results.

These declines are mostly due to a deterioration in the company's legacy businesses, evidenced by a 10.7% YoY decline in revenues for Q2 2024 even as Fiber Broadband, Lumen's only real growth driver, grew 14.6% YoY. The number is poised to grow significantly in light of these new deals, but at $181 million in total revenue this segment still only represents around 5% of total sales. Further, while these deals have big headline values like $5 billion and $7 billion, the timeframes can be quite long, some of them up to 20 years, so the actual revenue and earnings recognition will be spread out.

That said, perhaps the most important aspect of these wins for Lumen is that they will provide much-needed cash to cover the liquidity gap that had formed due to the erosion of legacy cash flow. Stansbury said as much on the Q2 2024 conference call:

So the key thing here is that, in one set of deals in those $5 billion deals that the net after-tax cash generated from that fully fund the liquidity gap that we've talked about for so long on these calls. It's over, it's behind us.

While revenue recognition is dispersed over the course of the contract, Lumen does receive cash upfront to facilitate the fiber build-out, which, in addition to inflows from continuing to divest non-core assets, will provide a lifeline as the company tries to de-leverage. As evidence of this, for the second quarter, Lumen reported FCF of -$156 million, but provided outlook for between $1 billion and $1.2 billion in FCF for FY2024, which is primarily because an overwhelming majority of the cash flows from these fiber contracts, around 90%, is delivered in the first 3-4 years. Of course, while capital expenditures and operating expenses will increase as well to enable these fiber build-outs, the cash infusion is massive for Lumen and alleviates some of the market concerns over liquidity risks.

While there are many tailwinds for Lumen at the moment, the stock's prospects are where I think things get a little bit murky. On the one hand, I think the AI wave represents a very real growth opportunity for Lumen over the next few years: data centers are becoming increasingly power hungry and will likely be built in more remote and rural places going forward, making interconnects like the ones Lumen offers essential, the demand from hyperscalers for Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs shows no signs of slowing, and creating new conduit networks is exceedingly difficult and cost-intensive.

On the other hand, Lumen doesn't yet possess any competitive advantage in building these networks other than that they already have the existing infrastructure in place. This is valuable, but does put a ceiling on the kind of growth we can expect since the only sure thing is that the company will be able to monetize existing conduits.

My bull case for LUMN is that the company utilizes the upfront cash and recurring inflows from these fiber contracts to de-leverage its balance sheet to the point where organic operating cash flows cover interest payments and CapEx without the need for new deals to cover expenses and that non-core assets continue to be divested to create a lean, primarily fiber network operator that benefits from steady ongoing demand for AI data center interconnects. I think, if this comes to fruition, LUMN will see a significant stock price appreciation, even on top of recent gains. However, there are still significant risks before that vision is achieved.

Risks

First, the margins on these fiber build-outs are fairly slim over the first few years of the contract. Much of the cash received will need to go towards the expenses of the projects themselves and cannot be used to pay down big chunks of debt. The long tails will be extremely high margin, but it will be many years before Lumen can coast and rake in the EBITDA and cash.

Second, while the company's financial position is much improved from these deals, the balance sheet is still a mess.

Price/book ratio is in the dumps for obvious reasons, and while I've seen some clamor over the low price/sales ratio, if those sales aren't generating cash then they're not of much use to Lumen. There is some macroeconomic relief coming in the form of lower interest rates from the Fed, which should allow the company to refinance some of its debt in addition to the refinancing it already completed last quarter, but the sheer magnitude of liabilities compared to cash inflows means risk to investors remains.

Third, with the share price rocketing higher, there have been, as there usually are, detractors. Kerrisdale Capital and Hedgeye have both weighed in on the short side in recent weeks, despite a significant reduction in short interest, likely due to the major short squeeze that followed the announcement of Lumen's fiber contract wins. With short interest now at around 6%, it represents less of a risk but is still something of which investors should be aware.

Investor Takeaway

Alright, so what's the bottom line? In my opinion, there is significant upside here for those willing to jump in, but the risks are significant as well. Lumen has secured substantial deals for the use of its existing conduit network in fiber interconnects for AI-enabled data centers, representing an inflection point for a company that looked headed for bankruptcy just a few months ago. The company's significant long-term debt position, the burden of its legacy businesses, and a valuation that has baked in a lot of assumed execution offset some of these tailwinds and are the main reason I'm not recommending a Buy at this time.

I would classify LUMN as a high-risk, high-reward opportunity that speculative investors could benefit from nibbling at, though I'd likely wait for a better entry point considering the current valuation. For now, I rate LUMN a Hold for most investors and a Speculative Buy for the risk-takers among you.

Thanks for reading!

data.title: Generative AI's Chartreuse Adoption Moment
data.description:
A wise investment adviser said that if you do not understand the macro, you'll get the rest wrong. The following information is meant to offer context alongside others' AI revelations. At a recent conference at the Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank, AI, big data, remote work and outsourcing were the topics presented. However, generative AI was the main theme that captured my attention, but it plays on a very broad stage. To start, AI has been around for decades, primarily used in automation and machine learning. What's new is generative AI, which is associated with large language models (LLMs). Prominent examples include OpenAI’s ChatGPT, Google’s Gemini and Meta’s Llama. According to scholars and leaders at the Fed, generative AI is expected to become a general-purpose technology, meaning it will be pervasive and lead to downstream innovation.

We’re seeing significant investment in AI, and around 1,700 AI models exist on the Microsoft Azure platform, as noted in one presentation. However, businesses are still experimenting with how to use generative AI—whether large, medium, or small firms. Data has been referred to as the "new oil," but high-quality data is acknowledged as becoming more scarce. One of the business leaders at the event highlighted the return on equity improvements in sectors like finance and banking due to AI adoption. The experimentation phase is evident across many sectors, with companies largely exploring AI to drive business process efficiencies.

(Video "After the Fed: Generative AI Macro Journey" highlights more Fed conference findings.)

Alongside LLMs, we now see small language models being developed for firms. A Forbes writer notes of Microsoft’s Phi-3.5: “This approach challenges the traditional view that bigger is always better in AI, potentially leading to a new era of AI development focused on sustainability and accessibility.”

The Phi-3.5 series represents a significant step forward in Microsoft’s AI research and development efforts. By focusing on creating smaller, more efficient models that can handle a wide range of tasks, Microsoft is addressing the growing demand for AI solutions that are both powerful and resource efficient.

Downstream

AI use in the oil and gas industry isn’t new, either. For roughly a decade, Palantir has been working with BP (BP) and now APA, formerly Apache, to find new efficiencies and market opportunities. BP began using Microsoft (MSFT) Copilot in November 2023. Further, since a 2019 announcement, BP has explored the use of machine learning and cognitive computing in its business with a system called Sandy.

Sandy was developed as a cloud-based geoscience platform using AI. BP says, “(Our) experts feed the platform geology, geophysics, reservoir and historic project information. It intuitively links that information together, identifying new connections and workflows, and creating a robust knowledge-graph of BP’s subsurface assets.” BP experts can then interrogate the data, like standard search, asking the knowledge-graph specific questions in natural language. The technology interprets results and performs rapid simulations.

In partnership with BP, Palantir’s AIP software will utilize large language models (LLMs) to improve and accelerate decision-making. They further state,

“Within the software, the robustness of the underlying data infrastructure and digital twin are combined with tools to ensure transparency into AI recommendations. This means that the software is designed to enable safe and reliable AI deployment and guard against hallucinations. Meanwhile, security features control what LLMs can and cannot do together with fully auditable digital records of any decisions or actions taken.”

And by the way, more recently announced, BP is strategically increasing oil and gas production. I noticed and discussed their slow “pivot” back after being a leading energy transition firm in past articles in December 2022 and later. (See video in article "Energy Transition Intricacies: Dynamics and Investment Themes" for BP color around minute 44:00. This article shows more of BP’s recent past strategy "Global Energy 2023: War Impacts, Decarbonization and Energy Security Premiums.") I point this out because it's a case of strategy not aligning with the macro. It happens, and in this case, Russia's war in Ukraine turned energy markets sideways as well.

Task Shift

Notably, AI remains complementary to human work. One academic from Columbia discussed the value of data in the investment management world, showing a 23% increase in data value between 2015 and 2019. Analysts who understand data bring this value to light, emphasizing the implication of the need for smart people to work with AI. Generative AI is freeing up time by automating mundane tasks, allowing workers to focus on higher-value activities that generate revenue — once they know how to use it.

The pace of technological advancements in machine learning is accelerating. From 1950 to 2010, scaling laws increased 1.4 times per year, but since 2010, it's over four times per year in the deep learning era. Siemens, a 170-year-old company managing one in three machines globally, presented about AI-enabled industrial automation, pairing AI with cloud services for advanced possibilities. (See chart further down.)

Platforms like Microsoft Azure and Nvidia are driving AI adoption in both different and overlapping ways. Platforms matter because they are where much of the value is being created, and where the competition is fierce. Generative AI is said to be transforming finance activities, and on the horizon is turning raw data into comprehensible visualizations. While some jobs are exposed to AI, particularly knowledge work like proofreading, web development, and even creative writing, the overall picture shows that many tasks will simply be enhanced by AI. It depends, however, on which seat one sits.

Relative to adoption, one professor discussed the productivity J-curve, noting that with new technologies, it takes roughly 40 years to realize transformational changes. For generative AI, this could span decades, with upfront investments leading to productivity gains in the long term. While we're still in the early stages, evidence suggests that generative AI is already delivering faster gains than expected, which is good news.

Digital Twinning

Siemens and Nvidia (in a separate event) both highlighted the use of digital twins — replicas of manufacturing facilities, for example. They're used before construction to optimize labor costs, energy use, and other factors. Nvidia’s sustainability officer noted:

“A partner, company Foxconn does a lot of electronics manufacturing based in Asia. They're opening a manufacturing facility in Mexico. They used our hardware coupled with some software from Siemens to create a digital twin, the manufacturing facility. They are projecting that the manufacturing facility will save 30% on energy because they used AI to optimize it on the front end.”

Transportation was cited as having the potential of a net positive impact across the board, given the prospects of optimization there. While the higher-end use case shows the potential for AI to drive efficiency, broader adoption is expected to take years. Although AI hiring surged last year, growth has now leveled off, indicating that diffusion across industries will be gradual.

Investment case

What's common to these business cases is the layering on of gen AI; it's additive in these industrial cases. It's also important to recognize that not every company needs large-scale AI like OpenAI’s ChatGPT. Firms are deciphering how to match the right technology to their workloads. Government and McKinsey studies show that AI adoption is rising, with 5.5% of firms using AI in the last two weeks and 75% of firms using AI in at least one business function this year, compared to 55% last year.

The idea of AI for everyone is noble. But just as Internet diffusion has taken decades, a "must-have" say some government policymakers, AI presently seems poised for enterprise-level adoption to a greater degree owing to investment costs. The consultancy speaking at the Fed said that costs of AI systems have decreased considerably, though.

As the market sorts through the devilish details and multibillion-dollar announcements, the due diligence process in investment selection could take a while. I still resort back to use cases to ultimately avoid loss of money and time. What type of AI is useful, worth the cost, and will generate value in excess of that cost? The AI hype is decreasing in degrees and giving a sober pause for thought. Chartreuse is greenish yellow — a cautionary green light.

data.title: The 21st Century Investor's Toolkit - 7 Trends
data.description:
Last year, I set out to rearrange my portfolio with one goal: Get exposure to the seven most important trends of the XXI century. Taking advantage of the process, I wanted to fix structural issues and revamp how I write and analyze articles.

I created two dummy portfolios from this initiative, The Cat and The Tortoise portfolios, to better convey the implications of risk profiles, time horizons, and other considerations. I will update you on new ideas for 2025 and the challenges ahead.

The 7 Trends of the XXI Century

Here, we will review the seven trends of the XXI century, explain why this represents an Alpha opportunity, and discuss how the picked stocks relate to this trend. We will also discuss challenges for 2025 and beyond.

Although each stock could impact several trends, I have limited the exposure to the two most significant for each one.

1. Technological Convergence Across Sectors (Well Covered): AI and high-speed communications will erode traditional sector boundaries. The global proliferation of the Internet further accelerates this trend, fostering interconnectedness across previously distinct domains.

The idea is that as sectors converge, some old moats holding well-defined sectors will erode. The strategy is to bet on the "siege weapons" that will be used to destroy these moats, and examine the moats we are betting on well.

Stocks in the trend: AMD (AMD), Nvidia (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL)

This selection is straightforward. AMD and Nvidia control the CPU and GPU market, and Alphabet applies these changes. Players like Qualcomm, TSM, and Intel could also be considered.

AMD and Nvidia were chosen because of the fabless approach, which allows for a more straightforward play on how technology evolves without exposure to the capital evolution and dynamics of companies that TSM has. To be clear, it is not that Fab exposure is undesirable in general, but that its implication dilutes the exposure to this trend, which is less "efficient."

2. Data Dilemma in IT (More Coverage Needed): The IT landscape faces a pivotal challenge in balancing privacy concerns against the invaluable benefits of data utilization. Real-time processing complexities loom ahead, requiring adept solutions, while the escalating significance of cybersecurity adds another layer of urgency.

The threat of cyberattacks and the importance of data privacy are increasing exponentially, especially with the flourishing of AI. Betting on companies that can provide protection or that will benefit from the increasing value of data privacy is key to benefiting from this trend.

Stocks in the trend: CrowdStrike (CRWD)

CrowdStrike is the only company in this trend. This clearly shows the portfolio is admittedly underexposed and needs to work on getting better exposure to it.

The challenge here is finding exciting and uncorrelated players (And then getting them at a reasonable price)

3. Integration of Virtual and Physical Realms (Trim and Maintain): The convergence of virtual and physical spaces will redefine how we perceive work, leisure, travel, and transportation. Innovative solutions will emerge, revolutionizing mass transit for enhanced speed, safety, enjoyment, and environmental sustainability.

The pandemic accelerated this trend substantially, showed us what was missing, and provided a - not so subtle - foreshadowing of what is coming in the century: greater reliance on virtual communications, a shift towards e-commerce or advanced last-mile deliveries, increasing value of digital goods, and greater automation of daily life.

Stocks in the trend: AMD, Nvidia, META (META), Amazon (AMZN), Airbnb (ABNB), Ouster (OUST), Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY), Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI), Netflix (NFLX)

This trend is arguably over-exposed. META would be the purest play here, as its biggest bet is literally to accomplish this. However, other players like AMD and Nvidia are key enablers of this trend. Less obvious plays are Ouster, which, in the long term, is exposure to autonomous vehicles, which would be key to enabling this integration, and in the short term, it has interesting exposures for the automation of the industry - Airbus, which plays into the increase in air travel for leisure and commuting. Applied Optoelectronics plays a key role in the high requirements of high-speed Internet and IoT, which are also key to this trend.

Netflix is a bit of an oddball here. It is the least direct play, and further evaluation will be made to determine whether it warrants inclusion in the portfolio.

4. Transformative Shifts in Demographics and Healthcare (Covered): As demographic conditions evolve, the healthcare industry will undergo a radical transformation propelled by technological and data-driven innovations. This will shift the demographics and economics of the world's social structure. Anticipated shifts in global population growth, distinct from the trends of the 20th century, will usher in new economic conditions and trends.

Medical advancements present an opportunity, and the consequence of longer lives and more active lives in later years will shift the healthcare structure and economics of pension plans, careers, and key decisions like the time of marriage, the number and timing of children, retirement plans, and wealth and its applications.

As a simplistic example, in the Middle Ages, where the average lifespan was about 30 years, concepts like a 30-year mortgage, having children after 30, retirement planning, and Child labor laws would be ludicrous. Yet, by the end of the XXI century, the world's life expectancy might increase by 20-40 years (depending on your level of optimism), considering the expansion of curable diseases in developing countries and advancements in medical treatments.

Stocks in the trend: META, MercadoLibre (MELI), Tyson (TSN), Duolingo (DUOL), Airbnb, Ouster, Airbus, DexCom (DXCM), Paysign (PAYS), CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP)

Let's divide this trend into three big exposures so it is clearer:

  • Health: CRSP, DXCM, and PAYS - healthcare will be changing a lot in this century. This will be largely due to the technology and drivers but also to how healthcare is funded and its core economics as a consequence of improving health. Here, the portfolio is relatively well covered with DexCom and CRISPR on the technological side, while only modestly covered by Paysign's shifts in healthcare economics.
  • Population needs: Tyson, Duolingo, Ouster - Food requirements and resources will change drastically as the population increases. Migration and talent crises paired with greater interconnection between countries will increase the value/need of languages, and the automation of these organizations will increase substantially. Here, Ouster plays a key Role.
  • Population desires: Meta, MercadoLibre, Eadsy ABNB - The Population outside poverty will decrease, and the middle class will grow. This will shift consumer spending, and companies that benefit from this shift will grow. Meta from the increase in reliance on social media, Mercado Libre for the excellent example of how noncash transactions will shift and easy, and Airbnb for the travel and pleasure aspects. This has some correlation with the previous trend.

5. Sustainability Challenges and Energy Dynamics (something is missing): Escalating demands on energy consumption prompts reevaluating sustainability practices. Innovations in production materials, waste disposal, and transformation processes are paramount - agile manufacturing technologies, reshaping how products are created, and redefining scalability limits.

This could be arguably the biggest trend of the seven. Energy consumption and efficiency will define the XXI century, and how these shifts and the speed at which they shift are key to profiting from this trend.

This trend has three significant aspects: energy dynamics, Water, and waste. Regarding energy, the expectation is that fossil fuels will decrease as the century progresses and renewable energy will increase steadily. To fill this gap in energy supply, nuclear energy will take a bigger role as it makes more sense from a capital, reliability, and even sustainability point of view.

Water scarcity and purification will increase in value; however, these technologies are uncommon to find as a pure-play, and instead, solutions that reduce water consumption might be better targets.

Waste management will be much more relevant, from packaging to industrial waste disposal, CO2 footprint, and air pollution. For example, Waste Management (WM) will be reviewed soon, and stocks that benefit from this trend would also need inclusion.

Stocks in the trend: Fluor (FLR), Aspen Aerogels (ASPN), Tyson Foods, Cameco (CCJ), Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (URNM)

First, reliance on nuclear energy will increase. We need to shift from coal to green energy quickly. Renewable energies are highly capital intensive, and the logical bridge for this is Nuclear energy (Cameco and URNM cover this section, and FLR covers both nuclear construction and investments in renewable energy).

Fossil fuel reliance will also directly shift transportation and vehicles. Here, Aspen aerogels cover its exposure to both oil (for the short term) and increase in EV vehicles in the medium term. The new battery type could be a key catalyst in the long term.

Tyson is the only stock betting on the sustainable aspect of the food chain, water scarcity, and CO2 effects. It is not a bad fit, but more exposure to water and CO2 is needed.

6. Transformation in the Infrastructure Industry (More Exposure needed): The infrastructure sector is on the brink of a significant transformation, witnessing the emergence of novel business models, products, and services. The traditional reliance on concrete and steel will give way to a more diversified range of materials, reflecting technological advancements.

From the eternal tease of Graphene solutions to large-scale 3D printing with new materials. This trend will radically change business models and could provide meaningful alpha.

Stocks in the trend: Aspen Aerogels and Applied Optoelectronics

New materials, new requirements. Aerogels are a great example of how new materials will change how we build and isolate, and the possibilities that better and smarter materials and new ways of construction will create.

Applied Optoelectronics will enable this change, supporting IoT shifts, new business models, and smarter buildings and infrastructure requirements. It is more of a tangential exposure.

Here, more exposure is needed, especially for new materials and the construction process that they require.

7. Increase of Entropy (The More, the Better): Societal disruptions, including pandemics, intensified climate change impacts, and migration, signify a new era characterized by new volatility patterns and correlations.

Playing this trend is better explained in Taleb's Antifragile book and less formally so by Madilyn Bailey's overrated song. Chaos will increase, so we have to rely on this to benefit from change. The portfolio has some strong positions, and others play directly into it, but overexposure to this trend is arguably impossible.

Stocks in the trend: Fluor, MercadoLibre, Amazon, Alphabet, TLH, SGOV, Paysign

Fluor - More hurricanes and disasters mean more construction, and the market underestimates this impact. The more chaotic the weather and energy demands become, the more engineering work would be required.

MercadoLibre - shifting economics in Latin America and change from cash to electronic payment, short-term investments, and product selection. MercadoLibre's leadership and services are fantastic examples of navigating through chaos and deserve a substantial spot on the trend.

Amazon is another animal. Complexities will increase its moat, and the AWS side means that the more reliance on cloud computing there is, the more this growth, which is a similar thesis to Alphabet, which arguably has a stronger moat and diversified opportunities.

TLH and SGOV are a play on the entropy of yield markets considering current high short-term yields and upcoming yield reduction from the fed, and finally,

Paysign is an economic buffer that, arguably, is plasma market gains during economic downturns.

Graphically

This is how the portfolio looks from a trend balance point of view. It is evident that Data dilemma and infrastructure are the most underexposed trends, and increasing this exposure will be one of the targets for the first half of 2025

A representation of each one of these positions as part of the total is also interesting, as it provides a clear picture of where the portfolio might be concentrating risk.

The parts not covered in the portfolio are options. I may generally disclose how I am using options (for example, to hedge AMD and Nvidia positions), but I feel giving greater detail on these aspects oversimplifies the complexities and risks of using options and the suitability and controls.

Having said that, arguably, AMD and NVDA represent concentrated positions in the portfolio, but I have found it very hard to reduce the percentages without compromising overall return or meaningful exposure to the trends.

Other challenges and objectives for 2025

With a portfolio reflecting my risk tolerance and exposure objectives, a big challenge of 2025 will be to write about the portfolio's results analysis, rebalancing, and risk measures. This would also imply a more comprehensive measurement of exposure to the seven trends and provide a more impartial context.

This would also apply to the Cat and Tortoise portfolios. During 2025, I will try to provide 2-4 updates on the portfolios, how they grow, and an analysis based on each profile.

The new dummy portfolio for 2025 will be the Dedgehog portfolio. This will focus on an imaginary hedgehog with a cryptocurrency-based portfolio trying to hedge it with stocks and ETFs.

I also want to improve the content's graphs and charts and provide other content forms. I love that Seeking Alpha now has the article as audio. I am now a TrendSpider Affiliate, which is one of the tools I will use to provide better charts and more comprehensive analysis. Thank you, sincerely, to all who reached out this year to provide insights, recommendations, insightful comments, and messages. I truly appreciate it.
